text: [{'text': "I'll systematically analyze the RTL/source files in the input scope for permission-related security vulnerabilities. Let me start by exploring the directory structure.", 'type': 'text'}, {'id': 'toolu_01XtVm7NbbqYkdiJsPjHPCKJ', 'caller': {'type': 'direct'}, 'input': {'path': '.'}, 'name': 'list_dir', 'type': 'tool_use'}]

[{'text': 'This is very important! I found a critical finding. Let me continue reading the CSR file and other files.', 'type': 'text'}, {'id': 'toolu_01UZW5N5g34B7HdxrezA5L6n', 'caller': {'type': 'direct'}, 'input': {'limit': 100, 'offset': 130, 'path': 'src/csr_regfile.sv'}, 'name': 'read_file', 'type': 'tool_use'}, {'id': 'toolu_01Pw6zA25uk4dp9hthTDU44S', 'caller': {'type': 'direct'}, 'input': {'path': 'src/rom2/rom2.sv'}, 'name': 'read_file', 'type': 'tool_use'}]

[{'text': 'Now let me do some targeted searches for critical security patterns.', 'type': 'text'}, {'id': 'toolu_01DkJEqmZDAAhq9n1j8J5uAw', 'caller': {'type': 'direct'}, 'input': {'path': '.', 'query': 'pass_chk'}, 'name': 'search_text', 'type': 'tool_use'}, {'id': 'toolu_01Miwa8bX8TKm2fEk4p4bjXP', 'caller': {'type': 'direct'}, 'input': {'path': '.', 'query': 'umode_o'}, 'name': 'search_text', 'type': 'tool_use'}]

{"analysis_summary": "Analysis of the HackaTDac19 JTAG/debug RTL source scope reveals multiple permission-related security vulnerabilities. The design implements a custom JTAG password authentication mechanism (DTM_PASS operation) in dmi_jtag.sv that gates read access to the debug module and sets a privilege-escalation signal (umode_o). This signal is routed to csr_regfile.sv where it unconditionally forces the processor privilege level to Machine mode (PRIV_LVL_M). Critical flaws include: (1) pass_chk is a combinational signal, never registered — so the authenticated state does not persist across clock cycles; (2) despite the guard on DTM_READ, DTM_WRITE access to the debug module is not protected by the password check at all; (3) the csr_regfile has a deliberate carve-out exempting CSR_MEPC from privilege level enforcement; and (4) the rom2 \"secure\" key store accepts AXI write transactions with no privilege enforcement, allowing software to overwrite the JTAG authentication key and AES keys stored in it.", "findings": [{"finding_id": "FIND-01", "status": "confirmed_finding", "summary": "JTAG password check uses a combinational (non-registered) pass_chk signal — authenticated state is lost every clock cycle", "vulnerability_category": "Authentication Bypass / Stateless Authorization", "affected_locations": [{"file": "src/debug/dmi_jtag.sv", "line_start": 79, "line_end": 79, "module": "dmi_jtag", "signal_or_register": "pass_chk"}, {"file": "src/debug/dmi_jtag.sv", "line_start": 114, "line_end": 120, "module": "dmi_jtag", "signal_or_register": "pass_chk"}, {"file": "src/debug/dmi_jtag.sv", "line_start": 177, "line_end": 182, "module": "dmi_jtag", "signal_or_register": "umode_o"}], "evidence": [{"file": "src/debug/dmi_jtag.sv", "line_start": 79, "line_end": 79, "module": "dmi_jtag", "object": "pass_chk", "evidence_type": "signal_declaration", "description": "pass_chk is declared as a plain 'logic' wire (no flip-flop), inside always_comb. It is set to 1'b1 inside the DTM_PASS branch only for the current combinational evaluation cycle.", "supports_claim": "The signal is never registered, so its value is transient and reverts to the default (undriven/0) in any cycle where the DTM_PASS branch is not being re-evaluated."}, {"file": "src/debug/dmi_jtag.sv", "line_start": 93, "line_end": 182, "module": "dmi_jtag", "object": "always_comb block", "evidence_type": "behavioral_logic", "description": "pass_chk is assigned inside always_comb at line 117. In the same always_comb, umode_o is driven based on pass_chk (lines 177-182). There is no always_ff register for pass_chk.", "supports_claim": "A combinational pass_chk means the authentication status cannot be reliably used to gate multi-cycle operations and is vulnerable to glitching or re-evaluation without re-supplying the correct password."}], "reasoning_summary": "The DTM_PASS operation sets pass_chk=1 only within a combinational always_comb block. Because pass_chk has no sequential register (no always_ff capturing its state), the authentication is not persistent. In the very next cycle or evaluation where the DTM_PASS path is not active, pass_chk falls back to its default (undriven/0), meaning umode_o returns to 0. This makes the authentication scheme ineffective for gating any multi-cycle or subsequent operation. Additionally, the condition on DTM_READ (line 110) requires pass_chk==1, but since it's combinational and can only be 1 during the same evaluation cycle as the DTM_PASS assignment, a DTM_READ issued in a subsequent JTAG transaction will always see pass_chk==0, effectively locking out authenticated reads. This is a design flaw that undermines the intended authentication.", "security_impact": "The authentication mechanism designed to control JTAG debug access is non-functional. Authenticated state does not persist, so the password gate on DTM_READ is always closed (after the initial cycle). Conversely, write access (DTM_WRITE, lines 112-113) is never gated by pass_chk at all — writes to the debug module proceed unconditionally regardless of authentication.", "confidence": "high", "uncertainty_or_missing_evidence": "The dm:: package enum definition for DTM_PASS is not present in the source scope; however, its use in context is unambiguous. The exact reset value of pass_chk as synthesized (to 0 or X) depends on tool, but RTL intent is clear.", "recommended_follow_up": ["Register pass_chk in an always_ff block so authentication state persists across JTAG transactions.", "Apply the pass_chk guard to DTM_WRITE as well as DTM_READ.", "Clear the registered pass_chk on reset or on JTAG trst_n assertion."]}, {"finding_id": "FIND-02", "status": "confirmed_finding", "summary": "JTAG DTM_WRITE operations bypass the password authentication check entirely", "vulnerability_category": "Missing Authorization / Privilege Bypass", "affected_locations": [{"file": "src/debug/dmi_jtag.sv", "line_start": 112, "line_end": 113, "module": "dmi_jtag", "signal_or_register": "state_d"}], "evidence": [{"file": "src/debug/dmi_jtag.sv", "line_start": 110, "line_end": 120, "module": "dmi_jtag", "object": "Idle state case branch", "evidence_type": "behavioral_logic", "description": "DTM_READ requires (pass_chk == 1'b1) at line 110. DTM_WRITE at line 112 has NO such guard — it immediately transitions to the Write state.", "supports_claim": "Any JTAG user can issue DTM_WRITE operations to program the debug module registers (including DMControl which controls hart halting, reset, etc.) without knowing the password."}], "reasoning_summary": "The design intent is to require password authentication before JTAG debug access. However, only DTM_READ checks pass_chk. The DTM_WRITE path (line 112-113) is entirely unconditioned by pass_chk. This means an unauthenticated JTAG user can write to any debug module CSR — including DMControl to halt/reset harts, Command to execute abstract commands, or ProgBuf to inject code — without authentication.", "security_impact": "An unauthenticated attacker with physical JTAG access can write arbitrary values to all debug module registers, potentially halting or resetting processor harts, injecting arbitrary code via the program buffer, or triggering system bus accesses to arbitrary memory addresses. This completely circumvents the intended JTAG password protection.", "confidence": "high", "uncertainty_or_missing_evidence": "None — the code path is direct and unambiguous.", "recommended_follow_up": ["Add the same (pass_chk == 1'b1) guard to the DTM_WRITE branch.", "Ensure pass_chk is a registered (sequential) signal to guarantee persistent authentication state."]}, {"finding_id": "FIND-03", "status": "confirmed_finding", "summary": "Successful JTAG password match unconditionally forces processor privilege to Machine Mode via umode_i signal in csr_regfile", "vulnerability_category": "Privilege Escalation", "affected_locations": [{"file": "src/csr_regfile.sv", "line_start": 938, "line_end": 938, "module": "csr_regfile", "signal_or_register": "priv_lvl_o"}, {"file": "src/debug/dmi_jtag.sv", "line_start": 177, "line_end": 182, "module": "dmi_jtag", "signal_or_register": "umode_o"}, {"file": "tb/ariane_testharness.sv", "line_start": 153, "line_end": 153, "module": "ariane_testharness", "signal_or_register": "ariane_umode"}, {"file": "tb/ariane_testharness.sv", "line_start": 603, "line_end": 603, "module": "ariane_testharness", "signal_or_register": "umode_i"}], "evidence": [{"file": "src/csr_regfile.sv", "line_start": 938, "line_end": 938, "module": "csr_regfile", "object": "priv_lvl_o assignment", "evidence_type": "signal_assignment", "description": "assign priv_lvl_o = (debug_mode_q || umode_i) ? riscv::PRIV_LVL_M : priv_lvl_q; — when umode_i is asserted, the reported privilege level is forced to M-mode regardless of the actual priv_lvl_q state.", "supports_claim": "Any entity that can assert umode_i can force the processor to appear as if operating at Machine privilege level, bypassing all lower-privilege access restrictions."}, {"file": "src/debug/dmi_jtag.sv", "line_start": 177, "line_end": 182, "module": "dmi_jtag", "object": "umode_o drive", "evidence_type": "behavioral_logic", "description": "umode_o is set to 1 whenever pass_chk==1 in the always_comb block. This creates a direct combinational path from password comparison to privilege escalation.", "supports_claim": "A JTAG operator that supplies the correct 32-bit password can escalate the processor to M-mode privilege — but since pass_chk is combinational, this path is glitch-prone and unreliable by design."}, {"file": "tb/ariane_testharness.sv", "line_start": 153, "line_end": 153, "module": "ariane_testharness", "object": "umode_o to ariane_umode connection", "evidence_type": "port_connection", "description": ".umode_o ( ariane_umode ) — the output of dmi_jtag is wired directly to ariane_umode which feeds into the processor CSR file.", "supports_claim": "Confirms the signal path from JTAG authentication to privilege level override is complete."}], "reasoning_summary": "The design adds a non-standard signal path where a JTAG password match (pass_chk) drives umode_o high, which is then connected to the processor's umode_i input. In csr_regfile.sv line 938, umode_i forces priv_lvl_o to PRIV_LVL_M. This is a dangerous privilege-escalation backdoor: any external JTAG interface that can observe or brute-force the 32-bit password gains the ability to force the processor into Machine mode privilege.", "security_impact": "If an attacker learns or brute-forces the JTAG password (only 32 bits, sourced from the lower 32 bits of rom2 slot 1), they can assert umode_i and force the entire processor into Machine privilege mode, bypassing all privilege-level-based access control for supervisor and user mode CSR accesses, memory protection enforcement, and potentially enabling access to M-mode-only system resources.", "confidence": "high", "uncertainty_or_missing_evidence": "The exact downstream effect on memory protection depends on how priv_lvl_o is used in the MMU and PMP, which are not in the provided source scope.", "recommended_follow_up": ["Remove the umode_i/umode_o backdoor signal path entirely.", "Do not couple JTAG authentication state to the processor's reported privilege level.", "If debug privilege elevation is needed, use the standard RISC-V debug mode mechanism (dcsr.prv)."]}, {"finding_id": "FIND-04", "status": "confirmed_finding", "summary": "rom2 'secure' key store accepts AXI write transactions with no access control, allowing unprivileged software to overwrite JTAG and AES keys", "vulnerability_category": "Missing Write Protection on Security-Sensitive Memory", "affected_locations": [{"file": "src/rom2/rom2.sv", "line_start": 36, "line_end": 43, "module": "rom2", "signal_or_register": "secure_reg"}, {"file": "tb/ariane_peripherals.sv", "line_start": 204, "line_end": 213, "module": "ariane_peripherals", "signal_or_register": "rom2_we"}], "evidence": [{"file": "src/rom2/rom2.sv", "line_start": 36, "line_end": 43, "module": "rom2", "object": "always_ff write path", "evidence_type": "behavioral_logic", "description": "When req_i && we_i, secure_reg[addr_i[...]] <= wdata_i; — writes to the secure key register array are gated only by req_i and we_i, with no privilege level check.", "supports_claim": "Any AXI master (including user-mode software) that can address the ROM2 peripheral region and issue a write transaction will overwrite keys stored in secure_reg."}, {"file": "tb/ariane_peripherals.sv", "line_start": 186, "line_end": 213, "module": "ariane_peripherals", "object": "axi2mem to rom2 wiring", "evidence_type": "port_connection", "description": "axi2mem converts AXI bus transactions to req/we/addr/wdata signals and feeds them directly to rom2 without any access control interposer. rom2_we is driven directly from the AXI slave.", "supports_claim": "The connection chain from the AXI bus to rom2's write port has no privilege filter."}, {"file": "tb/ariane_peripherals.sv", "line_start": 215, "line_end": 217, "module": "ariane_peripherals", "object": "key extraction assignments", "evidence_type": "signal_assignment", "description": "assign jtag_key = key_reg_out[1][31:0]; assign access_ctrl_reg[0] = key_reg_out[2][47:0]; assign access_ctrl_reg[1] = key_reg_out[3][47:0]; — the JTAG password, AES key, and access control registers all derive from writable secure_reg entries.", "supports_claim": "Overwriting rom2 entries directly corrupts the JTAG password used for authentication and the AES key fed to the AES accelerator."}], "reasoning_summary": "rom2 is described as a secure fuse-like key store containing AES keys (slot 0), the JTAG authentication key (slot 1), and access control registers (slots 2-3). However, the RTL shows that rom2 accepts write transactions (we_i) from the AXI bus without any privilege or access control check. The axi2mem bridge directly converts AXI write transactions to we_i pulses. Any bus master with access to the ROM2Base address range (0x0021_0000) can overwrite these keys. The module is named 'rom2' (read-only memory) but is functionally a writable RAM.", "security_impact": "Software running at any privilege level that can reach the ROM2 address range can: (1) overwrite the JTAG authentication password to a known value and then authenticate via JTAG; (2) overwrite the AES key, compromising data confidentiality; (3) overwrite access control registers, bypassing peripheral access control for all AXI masters. This is a complete compromise of the security architecture built around rom2.", "confidence": "high", "uncertainty_or_missing_evidence": "It is not visible in the provided scope whether the axi_xbar implements access_ctrl filtering that might restrict which privilege levels can access ROM2Base. The access_ctrl wiring is present (tb/ariane_testharness.sv line 450, 472) but the axi_node_intf_wrap implementation is not in scope.", "recommended_follow_up": ["Implement write-once (fuse-like) semantics: allow writes only on the first power-on reset, or never allow writes after reset.", "Add a privilege-level check in the AXI path to ROM2 — only M-mode accesses should be permitted.", "Rename the module to reflect its true read-write nature and add explicit security documentation.", "Consider moving key loading to a one-time hardware copy from actual fuse hardware on reset with no software-accessible write path."]}, {"finding_id": "FIND-05", "status": "confirmed_finding", "summary": "CSR privilege level check has a deliberate bypass for CSR_MEPC, allowing any privilege level to read/write the Machine Exception PC register", "vulnerability_category": "Privilege Level Enforcement Bypass (CSR Access Control)", "affected_locations": [{"file": "src/csr_regfile.sv", "line_start": 853, "line_end": 857, "module": "csr_regfile", "signal_or_register": "csr_exception_o"}], "evidence": [{"file": "src/csr_regfile.sv", "line_start": 853, "line_end": 857, "module": "csr_regfile", "object": "CSR privilege check condition", "evidence_type": "behavioral_logic", "description": "Line 854: if ((riscv::priv_lvl_t'(priv_lvl_o & csr_addr.csr_decode.priv_lvl) != csr_addr.csr_decode.priv_lvl) && !(csr_addr.address==riscv::CSR_MEPC)) — the original check (commented out on line 853) did not have the CSR_MEPC exception. The modified version adds '&& !(csr_addr.address==riscv::CSR_MEPC)' to suppress the illegal-instruction exception for MEPC accesses from lower privilege levels.", "supports_claim": "This carve-out allows supervisor-mode or user-mode code to access CSR_MEPC (a Machine-mode register, address 0x341) without raising an illegal instruction exception."}, {"file": "src/csr_regfile.sv", "line_start": 853, "line_end": 853, "module": "csr_regfile", "object": "Commented-out original check", "evidence_type": "code_comment", "description": "The original correct privilege check is commented out on line 853, and replaced with the modified version on line 854, confirming this is an intentional change.", "supports_claim": "The bypass was deliberately introduced."}], "reasoning_summary": "CSR_MEPC (Machine Exception Program Counter, address 0x341) is an M-mode register that should only be accessible from Machine privilege level. The standard RISC-V privilege check (lines[11:10] of CSR address encode minimum privilege) would raise an illegal instruction for S-mode or U-mode accesses. The modified condition on line 854 explicitly suppresses this exception for CSR_MEPC. This allows supervisor or user mode software to read or write the Machine EPC register, which stores the address that will be jumped to on MRET. Writing MEPC from S/U-mode could redirect machine-mode exception returns to attacker-controlled code.", "security_impact": "Supervisor or user mode code can write CSR_MEPC to an arbitrary address. If the system subsequently executes MRET (e.g., during an interrupt or exception return), the processor will jump to the attacker-controlled address with Machine privilege, achieving arbitrary code execution at M-mode. This is a privilege escalation vulnerability.", "confidence": "high", "uncertainty_or_missing_evidence": "The effect depends on whether there are practical scenarios where MRET is executed after an S/U-mode write to MEPC. This depends on OS and firmware behavior not visible in the RTL scope.", "recommended_follow_up": ["Remove the '&& !(csr_addr.address==riscv::CSR_MEPC)' exception from the privilege check.", "Restore the original commented-out privilege check on line 853.", "Audit all other CSR privilege checks for similar intentional bypasses."]}], "no_finding_reason": "N/A — multiple confirmed findings were identified.", "global_uncertainty": "The axi_node_intf_wrap (AXI crossbar with access_ctrl) implementation is not present in the source scope, so it is unknown whether the access control registers from rom2 slots 2-3 are actually enforced at the bus fabric level. If they are correctly enforced, the severity of FIND-04 may be partially mitigated (only M-mode could write ROM2), but the write vulnerability in rom2 itself and all other findings remain. The dm:: package (defining DTM_PASS, DTM_READ, DTM_WRITE enum values) is not in scope; their values are inferred from context. The full ariane_pkg and riscv_pkg definitions are also not in scope, so PRIV_LVL_M encoding is assumed standard (2'b11)."}
